var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'Aging Research' passage tells us that researchers prefer young mice because of grant cycles and publishing pressure — funding constraints distort the field. That matches B: financial restrictions are problematic for the study of aging.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is argued here?' — a main-argument question. Read the whole 'Aging Research' paragraph once and ask: what does the passage WANT you to conclude about aging research? Watch out for options that name a true side detail but miss the argument.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the argument. First, the diagnosis from Sierra: 'There's a lot of grumbling… about how often we cure diseases in mice that never translates when we try those cures in humans.' Then the cause: 'Since most grants are usually only for a four- to five-year period, using younger mice is not only less expensive, but it avoids the delay of waiting for an animal to age.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Aging research suffers because the grant system runs in 4-5 year cycles. To publish and renew funding on that clock, researchers use young mice rather than wait for old ones — even though aging is what they are supposed to be studying. Money pressure is bending the science.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Clinical trials' = tests of a treatment on people. 'Grant' = a fixed sum of research money awarded for a fixed period. 'Translate' here is the lab-to-clinic kind: a result that works in mice 'translates' if it also works in humans. The passage's complaint is that mouse-cures often DON'T translate.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: B says 'Financial restrictions in the research process are problematic for the study of aging.' That maps directly onto the grant-cycle sentence: short funding windows force young-mouse studies, which then fail to translate. The argument IS financial-pressure-distorts-aging-research.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The insight in one sentence: an ELF main-argument question rewards the option that names the CAUSE the passage diagnoses, not the symptoms (failed translation, mouse choice) it lists along the way.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'aging research may be on the rise' as a generally optimistic frame and assume mouse studies are CONTRIBUTING to human knowledge.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's whole point is the opposite — mouse-cures 'never translate' to humans. Step 3 nails it: financial pressure makes researchers use young mice precisely BECAUSE of the grant clock, and that breaks the translation. A inverts the argument.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'waiting for an animal to age' and conclude mice don't live long enough — so the field needs longer-lived animals.
distractors.why_wrong: The issue is not mouse lifespan as a biological limit; it is that researchers AVOID waiting for the mice they already have to grow old. Step 2's grant-cycle sentence makes the cause economic, not biological. C names a problem the passage never raises.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'financial restrictions' is to assume funding for human-aging studies is scarce.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage talks about how grant LENGTH shapes ANIMAL choice — not about scarcity of funding for human work. Step 5's match is specifically with the four-to-five-year grant cycle distorting study design, not with unmet funding demand.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-031.technique: Cause-vs-symptom pattern: when an ELF passage lists a complaint (mouse-cures fail to translate) AND then names a structural reason (short grant cycles), the main-argument answer almost always points at the STRUCTURAL CAUSE. Trigger to memorise: 'symptom-then-cause sentence pair → answer is the cause'.
var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: Vivid quotations from a named expert ('There's a lot of grumbling…') pull your attention to the symptom they describe. The argument lives in the next sentence — the structural diagnosis — not in the quote itself.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The 'Telling Time' passage walks through prehistoric, Egyptian, Greek and Roman timekeeping, noting Roman hours stretched and shrank with the seasons and the fixed 60-minute hour only arrived in the 14th century. That matches D: timekeeping conventions have varied throughout history.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is said here about timekeeping?' — a content-summary question. Read the paragraph and ask: what general claim about timekeeping does the passage support across ALL the examples it gives? Distractors will usually overreach on one example.

steps.title: Locate the structural sentences
steps.text: Three structural beats matter. The Egyptian sundial-and-star-clock setup is the start. Then Roman practice: 'an hour was longer (about 75 minutes) in the summer than in the winter (45 minutes).' Then the closure: 'The standardized 60-minute hour only arrived in the 14th century.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Different eras measured time differently. Egyptians used sundials and star-charts. Greeks added water-clocks and sand-timers. Romans stretched the hour with the seasons. Only in the 14th century did a fixed 60-minute hour appear. Bottom line: there is no single historical timekeeping standard — practice has shifted.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Sundial' = a flat surface with a stick whose shadow moves as the Sun crosses the sky. 'Water-clock' = a vessel that drips at a known rate so the water level marks elapsed time. 'Calibrated to the Earth's polar axis' = aligned with the line the Earth spins around, so the shadow movement stays consistent across seasons. None of this matters for the right answer — the point is just that methods varied.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: D says 'Timekeeping conventions have varied throughout history' — the variation is exactly what the paragraph documents (different instruments, seasonal Roman hours, late arrival of the 60-minute hour). The passage doesn't claim uniformity, doesn't credit one inventor, and doesn't compare ancient accuracy to modern accuracy.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The insight in one sentence: when a paragraph LISTS varied historical practices on one topic, the right answer is usually the broadest 'practice has varied' option — the specific examples are evidence, not the headline.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'In ancient times, timekeeping followed different basic principles around the world' is yes — the passage gives examples from different places.
distractors.why_wrong: The examples (Egypt, Greece, Rome) are a CHRONOLOGY of European/Mediterranean practice, not a comparison ACROSS regions at one time. The passage never contrasts what 'around the world' did simultaneously, so 'different basic principles around the world' overreaches geographically.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'sundials, water-clocks and sand-timers provided more accuracy' as a claim that the Greeks invented modern timekeeping.
distractors.why_wrong: Step 3's paraphrase shows the Greek line is just one stop on a longer chronology — the 60-minute hour arrives nearly two thousand years later, possibly thanks to Ibn Al-Shatir. B treats one waypoint as the destination.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the Roman 75-vs-45-minute hour as 'wildly imprecise', C — 'Ancient timekeeping was extremely inaccurate' — sounds right.
distractors.why_wrong: The seasonal Roman hour wasn't an error; it was an intentional convention. Step 5 reminds you the passage describes varying STANDARDS, not failed attempts at a fixed standard. Calling them inaccurate imports a modern yardstick the text doesn't endorse.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-032.technique: List-of-examples pattern: when a paragraph walks through historical examples on one topic, the right answer is usually the structural summary ('practice has varied / evolved / shifted') rather than a claim tied to any single example. Trigger to memorise: 'chronology of practices → variation answer'.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The opening of 'Bitter Pills' is a frame: the British public loves the NHS, but the institution is now 'looking frail' — budget overruns, doctor strikes, a £20 billion funding gap. That matches C: there is great public attachment to the NHS, but its current state is troubling.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is argued about the NHS in the beginning of the text?' — note 'in the beginning'. You're looking for the opening's main argument, NOT the cure-side prescriptions that come later in the article. Confine yourself to the first two paragraphs.

steps.title: Locate the opening's two beats
steps.text: The opening is built on a contrast. Beat one — loyalty: 'Most retain an almost filial loyalty… politicians are less likely to talk of improving the NHS than "protecting" it.' Beat two — trouble: 'Yet this national treasure is looking frail. Nine out of ten of the local trusts… are spending beyond their budgets; overall, the service faces a funding gap of £20 billion by the end of the decade.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Britons love the NHS — they want it 'protected', not just improved. But the institution itself is in bad shape: most local trusts overspend, doctors are striking, hospitals are shutting children's emergency departments, and there is a huge funding gap on the horizon. So: deep affection, but real trouble.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Filial loyalty' = the kind of loyalty children feel toward a parent. 'Local trust' = a regional NHS administrative body that runs hospitals on a budget. 'Spending beyond their budgets' = running deficits. The 'protecting' in scare quotes signals that the article finds the political instinct to merely defend, rather than reform, naive.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: C says 'There is great public attachment to the NHS but its current state is troubling.' That's the two-beat opening exactly: loyalty + frailty. The other options each lean on one beat while contradicting the other.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The insight in one sentence: opening-paragraph questions on contrast-built ELF passages reward the option that names BOTH halves of the contrast — discount any option that captures only one.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'national treasure' as evidence that 'economic problems have not so far affected its medical quality' — affection plus the word 'treasure' suggests continued excellence.
distractors.why_wrong: The opening explicitly names quality problems: a children's emergency department was shut 'because it was found to be unsafe'; trusts may delay surgery on obese patients. Step 2's second beat shows quality IS being affected, so A contradicts the text.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the funding gap and doctor strikes and conclude the NHS faces collapse if it doesn't reform fast.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Will collapse' is a stronger claim than the opening makes. The text uses 'looking frail' and 'struggling to make ends meet' — diagnostic language, not catastrophic. Step 3's paraphrase deliberately stops at 'real trouble', not collapse. B overreaches.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the line that politicians talk of 'protecting' the NHS rather than improving it, D — that politicians prefer the status quo — sounds plausible.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'protecting' line is one detail used to characterise public attitudes, not the argument. Step 5 keeps the focus on the contrast that organises the whole opening; D promotes a detail into the headline and ignores the loyalty + frailty pairing.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-033.technique: Two-beat contrast pattern: when an ELF opening sets up 'X is loved BUT X is in trouble', the main-argument answer must name BOTH halves. Trigger to memorise: 'opening paragraph + sentence starting with Yet/But/However → contrast answer'.
var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: Scope discipline: the question said 'in the beginning of the text'. Solutions discussed in later paragraphs (efficiency, prevention, fees) are tempting bait but out of scope for an opening-paragraph question.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The article's prescription for the NHS is twofold: more efficiency on the supply side AND more money from taxpayers + new charges on the demand side. That matches B: a combination of taxes, efficiency improvements and rationing is needed.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is the basic idea presented for solving the NHS's problems?' — a solution-design question. You need the option that captures the SHAPE of the fix the article proposes, not any single component of it.

steps.title: Locate the prescription sentences
steps.text: The fix is laid out across the middle of the article. Supply side: 'Increasing the NHS's capacity will require a far more ruthless focus on efficiency.' Demand side: 'taxpayers will have to get used to forking out more' and 'Managing demand will involve… toxic ones such as introducing charges for services that have been free.' The closer crystallises it: 'Higher taxes, new charges and more rationed services.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The article says no single lever will work. You need three at once: (1) run the system more efficiently (close small hospitals, scale up, share back-office); (2) raise more money (higher taxes, new fees for things that used to be free); (3) ration — divert patients from expensive to cheaper care, charge for in-person visits, focus on prevention. Three levers, used together.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Rationing' here means deliberately limiting demand or steering it to cheaper alternatives — not running out of supplies. 'Subjecting services to fees' = charging for visits/procedures that used to be free. 'Ruthless focus on efficiency' is the article's phrase for closing under-performing small hospitals in favour of bigger specialist ones.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: B says 'a combination of taxes, efficiency improvements and rationing is needed' — and the closing sentence literally lists 'Higher taxes, new charges and more rationed services' as the 'bitter pills'. The match is almost word-for-word. The other three options each capture at most one lever or invent one the article rejects.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The insight in one sentence: when an ELF passage explicitly lists multiple parts of a fix ('higher taxes, new charges and more rationed services'), the right answer must echo the list — single-lever options are traps.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'the focus must shift away from treating illness towards preventing it' and conclude the basic idea is reorienting from treatment to age-related prevention.
distractors.why_wrong: Prevention is named as one element, not the basic idea. Step 3's three-lever paraphrase shows efficiency, money and rationing are all required — A picks one lever and elevates it. The article never says treatment should give way to prevention; it says prevention is cheaper to add.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'introducing charges for services that have been free' and conclude the basic idea is to introduce user fees and rationing — without taxes.
distractors.why_wrong: The article explicitly couples charges WITH tax rises: 'taxpayers will have to get used to forking out more.' Step 2's two-side prescription shows you can't subtract taxes from the fix. C drops the tax lever.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'a budget based on the population they serve' and 'private outfits can do this with a profit margin to spare', D — privatisation-based reform — sounds close.
distractors.why_wrong: The population-budget model is a payment-reform suggestion within the public system, not a privatisation programme. The 'private outfits' line is a throwaway concession ('good for them'), not the article's basic idea. Step 5 disqualifies D for naming a lever the article only nods at.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-034.technique: Multi-lever solution pattern: when an ELF passage frames a problem as needing several simultaneous fixes and even lists them in a closing summary sentence, the right answer is the multi-lever option. Trigger to memorise: 'closer sentence with comma-separated fixes → combined-fix answer'.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: The article argues that the NHS still wastes money through fragmentation — fax machines, attachment to small local hospitals, scattered surgical programmes — and that scaling up to larger units improves outcomes. That matches A: larger health care units tend to give clear advantages.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is claimed concerning the NHS in relation to efficiency and treatment quality?' — a claim-finding question with TWO axes you have to satisfy at once: efficiency AND treatment quality. The right option must speak to both.

steps.title: Locate the efficiency-and-quality sentences
steps.text: The relevant block is the inefficiency paragraph. Key sentences: 'the English have a romantic attachment to small local hospitals, which are costlier AND deliver worse results than big specialist ones'; 'By scaling up, the NHS could offer better care for the same money'; 'Countries such as Germany and Denmark have found that by reducing the number of hospitals that offer particular surgical procedures, they can reduce the incidence of complications.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Small local hospitals cost more AND produce worse results. Big specialist hospitals do the opposite — same money, better care. Germany and Denmark already concentrate surgical procedures and get fewer complications. So: bigger units win on both axes the question is asking about.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Cottage practices' = small local doctor's offices, often single-doctor. 'Back-office functions' = the admin work behind care (billing, scheduling, call centres) — not the doctor-patient bit. 'Incidence of complications' = the rate at which something goes wrong after surgery. The 'romantic attachment' phrase is mildly critical: the article thinks the public's love of small hospitals is sentimental rather than rational.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: A says 'Larger health care units tend to give clear advantages' — which is exactly the scaling-up argument: cheaper AND better. The two axes match. The other options each focus on one axis or invert the article's claim about size.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The insight in one sentence: questions that bundle two criteria (efficiency AND quality) reward the option that speaks to both — single-axis options lose, even when each axis is individually true.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'fax machines' and 'expensive overtime' and conclude the article wants better technology adoption to fix efficiency.
distractors.why_wrong: Technology comes up only as one example of waste, and the new-tech mention later is about patient self-management, not the central efficiency argument. Step 3 shows the article's structural claim is about HOSPITAL SIZE, not technology. B picks an example over the headline.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'the English have a romantic attachment to small local hospitals' is that the article praises tradition or argues local services should be protected.
distractors.why_wrong: The article uses 'romantic attachment' critically — it ARGUES AGAINST small local hospitals because they are 'costlier and deliver worse results than big specialist ones'. Step 2 makes this explicit. C reverses the article's actual position on small units.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Britain already spends less as a share of its GDP on health care than most other rich countries' and conclude the NHS is uniquely efficient by international standards.
distractors.why_wrong: That sentence is about underfunding, not efficiency. The article calls the NHS 'lean by international standards' but in the very same sentence says it 'still bleeds money through inefficiency'. So leanness ≠ efficiency. Step 2's quote pair separates the two. D collapses them.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-035.technique: Two-axis question pattern: when an ELF prompt names two criteria with 'and' (here 'efficiency AND treatment quality'), the right answer must satisfy both. Trigger to memorise: 'X and Y in the prompt → option that names a single mechanism that wins on both'.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: The article argues that hospital beds are being used as substitutes for old people's homes because social-care budgets have been slashed — health and social care need to be looked at together, not separately. That matches C: the demands on the two systems are interrelated.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is implied about health care in relation to social needs?' Two words to mark: 'implied' (the answer may not be stated word-for-word) and 'in relation to' (you need a CONNECTION between health care and social needs, not a claim about either alone).

steps.title: Locate the connection sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the link. 'One reason that hospital beds are in such short supply is that budgets for social care have been slashed. It makes no sense to use hospitals as expensive substitutes for old people's homes.' Then the structural fix: 'Amalgamating health and social care, as some regions are already doing, would lead to a more sensible allocation of resources.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: When social care is underfunded, elderly people who could be living in care homes end up stuck in hospital beds instead. So a problem in social care shows up as a problem in health care — the two systems are pushing on each other. Merging their budgets ('amalgamation') would let resources flow where they're actually needed.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Social care' here is the British term for community support services for elderly or disabled people — care homes, home visits, day centres — distinct from medical 'health care'. 'Amalgamating' = combining into one. 'Allocation of resources' = how money and staff get distributed across competing needs.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: C says 'the demands on the two systems are interrelated' — and the passage's claim is exactly that: a shortfall in one system creates demand in the other. Hospitals doing the work of care homes is the article's emblematic example.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The insight in one sentence: 'implied' + 'in relation to' questions reward an option that names a STRUCTURAL LINK between two things — claims about either thing in isolation will lose to a relational option.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'budgets for social care have been slashed' is that social services routinely get LESS money than health care.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Slashed' means cut from some prior level — it doesn't say social care now receives more than health care. Step 2's quote shows the article is making a relational point, not a comparative one about which budget is bigger. A invents a comparison the text doesn't make.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'a more sensible allocation of resources' as 'social services would benefit from medical professionals' input' — a borrowing-of-expertise idea.
distractors.why_wrong: Amalgamation is about combining budgets and patient pathways, not about medical staff advising social-care professionals. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the focus on resource flow. B narrows the article's claim to a specific staffing model it never mentions.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'old people's homes' and conclude the elderly should rely on family rather than the state.
distractors.why_wrong: The article wants the STATE to deliver social care better, not less. The implication is that under-resourced state social care pushes elderly people into hospital beds, not into family homes. Step 5 rules out D as importing a moral claim about family duty the article never makes.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-036.technique: Cross-system pattern: when an ELF passage explains a problem in System A by pointing to a shortfall in System B, 'implied' questions reward the option that names the LINKAGE between the two systems. Trigger to memorise: 'A's problem caused by B → relational answer'.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: After diagnosing the problems and prescribing the fixes, the article closes by saying the alternatives — including doing nothing — are worse: the NHS faces 'only decline' if politicians keep promising to 'protect' it without reforming. The piece is critical but constructive. That matches B: despite some difficult issues, it is basically on the right track.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What overall conclusion concerning the NHS is drawn in the text?' — note 'overall conclusion'. You need the writer's net verdict, balancing the dark diagnosis with the constructive prescription. Read the closing paragraph carefully — that is where the conclusion lives.

steps.title: Locate the closing verdict
steps.text: The last paragraph is the verdict: 'Higher taxes, new charges and more rationed services: these are bitter pills for politicians. But the English are ageing, and as long as their leaders promise simply to "protect" the NHS by doing nothing, the service faces only decline.' The whole article is saying: the cure is hard, but it exists; not curing leads to decline.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: The writer believes the NHS can be saved. The needed fixes are politically painful (higher taxes, fees, rationing) but they are real fixes. What is fatal is the political instinct to 'protect' without changing anything — that path ends in decline. So: the NHS has problems, but a workable plan exists, and the writer endorses staying on the taxpayer-funded model.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Bitter pill' = an unpleasant truth or measure that has to be accepted. 'On the right track' = heading in a sensible direction overall. The article's closing rhetoric — naming the medicine, warning against doing nothing — is the rhetoric of a sympathetic critic, not a pessimist.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: B says 'Despite some difficult issues, it is basically on the right track.' That captures the tone exactly: yes, problems exist (the diagnosis), but the right model is in place and the right reforms are identifiable (the prescription). The other options each push the verdict too far in one direction.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The insight in one sentence: an 'overall conclusion' question on an article that DIAGNOSES + PRESCRIBES rewards the moderate option — the writer is grading on the existence of a workable plan, not on the severity of the current pain.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'looking frail', 'funding gap', 'doctor strikes' and the final 'only decline' as a flat prediction that the NHS will deteriorate, full stop.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'only decline' line is conditional — it kicks in IF politicians keep 'doing nothing'. Step 3's paraphrase preserves the conditional structure. A drops the condition and reports the worst-case outcome as the writer's prediction.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Higher taxes, new charges and more rationed services' and conclude the writer wants a totally new funding model for the NHS.
distractors.why_wrong: The article endorses keeping 'the taxpayer-funded model' — it just wants more revenue and better targeting WITHIN that model. Step 2's closing quote spells this out. C overstates the radicalism of the writer's prescription.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'national treasure' and 'filial loyalty', D — that the NHS is widely admired and continues to provide excellent care — sounds plausible as a positive overall verdict.
distractors.why_wrong: The article ARGUES with the public's complacent affection: it says the NHS is loved but failing and needs reform. Step 5 disqualifies D for ignoring the diagnosis half of the diagnose-and-prescribe structure. The verdict is sympathetic-critical, not celebratory.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-037.technique: Diagnose-and-prescribe verdict pattern: when an ELF article spends most of its length naming problems AND a workable cure, the 'overall conclusion' answer is the moderate 'difficult-but-on-track' option. Trigger to memorise: 'diagnosis + prescription article → moderate verdict, not the worst-case option'.
var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: Final-line bait: the closing 'only decline' phrase is rhetorically scary and pulls readers toward pessimistic options. Reading the closer in context (it is conditional, not predictive) keeps you on the moderate verdict.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The 'Hanging Gardens' passage says no certain evidence has been recovered, the story may have conflated tales of earlier gardens, and Babylon's Gardens are the only one of the Seven Wonders whose existence remains unknown. That matches B: there are few clear indications that they have ever existed.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is said here concerning the Hanging Gardens of Babylon?' — a content-summary question. You're looking for the option the short paragraph DIRECTLY supports, watching for distractors that push past what the text says.

steps.title: Locate the evidence sentences
steps.text: Three sentences carry the claim. First: 'Yet no certain evidence of any gardens, hanging or otherwise, has been recovered from the Ancient Mesopotamian city.' Second: 'It is possible the story conflated tales of earlier gardens… although this hasn't been confirmed.' Third: 'To date, the Hanging Gardens remain the only one of the original Seven Wonders of the Ancient World whose location and very existence remain unknown.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Nebuchadnezzar's Hanging Gardens are the only one of the Seven Wonders that nobody has been able to confirm. There's no archaeological evidence in Babylon. There's a guess that the story might come from confusion with other gardens (like Nineveh's), but even that guess isn't confirmed. Net: very little to suggest they actually existed.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Conflated' = mixed up or merged together (two stories blended into one). 'Sixth century BC' = around 600-500 BC; 'seventh century BC' = around 700-600 BC, so 'earlier' than Nebuchadnezzar's reign. 'Mesopotamian city' = the area of modern Iraq; Babylon was its most famous city. None of these details settle the question — the headline is just the absence of evidence.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: B says 'There are few clear indications that they have ever existed' — which is a careful, scope-respecting paraphrase of 'no certain evidence' + 'location and very existence remain unknown'. The match is on the word 'few' (the passage allows for the legend itself as a weak indication, while denying anything firm).

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The insight in one sentence: short ELF passages on archaeology reward the option whose certainty level matches the text — the answer says 'few clear indications', the text says 'no certain evidence', and that's the right calibration.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'the story conflated tales of earlier gardens' as a claim that the Hanging Gardens themselves were actually built LATER than originally assumed.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage's hypothesis is the opposite — that the legend may have borrowed from EARLIER (seventh-century BC) gardens at Nineveh, not that Nebuchadnezzar's gardens were constructed later. Step 2's second quote is the key; A inverts the chronological direction.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the conflation hypothesis ('story conflated tales of earlier gardens') and conclude the Gardens 'were clearly not associated with Nebuchadnezzar II'.
distractors.why_wrong: The conflation is offered as a possibility, explicitly 'not confirmed'. The passage doesn't reject the Nebuchadnezzar association — it just notes a competing hypothesis. Step 3 keeps the wording tentative ('possible', 'unconfirmed'). C upgrades a possibility to 'clearly'.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'no certain evidence' and 'location and very existence remain unknown', D — 'overwhelming evidence that they are fictional' — feels like a natural strong statement of the same idea.
distractors.why_wrong: Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. The passage says nobody has found them; it does NOT say there is positive evidence they are fictional. Step 5's calibration argument fits B's 'few clear indications' but not D's 'overwhelming evidence'. D overreaches by flipping the burden of proof.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-038.technique: Evidence-calibration pattern: when an ELF passage uses careful negative language ('no certain evidence', 'remains unknown'), the right answer matches that level of certainty. Trigger to memorise: 'careful negatives in the text → careful negatives in the option ('few', 'no clear indications') — not 'overwhelming' or 'clearly''.
var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: Absence-of-evidence vs evidence-of-absence: a passage saying 'we haven't found X' is NOT saying 'X doesn't exist'. Options that translate the former into the latter are classic ELF traps.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The 'Dyspnea' passage names several easily identified causes of shortness of breath (asthma, lung infections, COPD, heart failure) but then says patients without any of those known conditions are 'out of luck' for diagnosis. So some causes are well understood, others aren't. That matches C: only some of the underlying causes for dyspnea are well understood.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What can be concluded here?' — a 'conclude' question, which means a claim that fits the FULL paragraph, not just one half. Watch for options that capture only the 'known causes' half or only the 'unknown causes' half.

steps.title: Locate the two-half structure
steps.text: Two halves split the paragraph. Known side: 'A number of easily identified disorders can cause such shortness of breath (dyspnea, in technical terms), including asthma, lung infections and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Congestive heart failure… is also well known to disrupt breathing.' Unknown side: 'But absent any of those conditions, patients who are out of breath are also out of luck in terms of getting an accurate diagnosis – or an effective treatment.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Doctors know what causes dyspnea SOMETIMES — asthma, lung infections, COPD, heart failure. But when none of those is the cause, doctors can't tell what's wrong, and they can't treat it. So: partial understanding — well-mapped for some causes, blank for others.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Dyspnea' = the medical term for shortness of breath (the paragraph defines it parenthetically). 'Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)' = a long-term lung disease, mainly seen in smokers. 'Congestive heart failure' = the heart can't pump strongly enough. 'Out of luck' is the article's casual way of saying 'no good options exist'.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: C says 'Only some of the underlying causes for dyspnea are well understood' — which is the paragraph's two-half structure compressed into one sentence: SOME causes are known and easy to diagnose, OTHERS leave patients without diagnosis or treatment. The 'only some' captures the partiality exactly.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The insight in one sentence: 'conclude' questions on two-half paragraphs reward the option that honours BOTH halves — 'only some' / 'partly' / 'in some cases but not others' wording usually wins.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'out of breath are also out of luck in terms of getting an accurate diagnosis' as a claim that dyspnea-related disorders are getting harder to treat over time.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes a steady-state problem, not a trend. It never says treatment is getting WORSE — only that some causes remain unknown. Step 3's paraphrase makes this static. A imports a temporal claim ('increasingly difficult') the text doesn't support.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct, given the careful catalogue of known causes, is that research is closing in on the unexplained cases and will soon explain them.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph offers zero forward-looking research claim. It describes what is known and what isn't, full stop. Step 5's calibration argument rules out B for adding optimism the text doesn't give. 'Out of luck' is the OPPOSITE of 'close to finding'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'easily identified disorders' and 'no accurate diagnosis or effective treatment' and combine them: easy to diagnose AND difficult to cure.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'easily identified' applies to ASTHMA-LIKE causes, while the 'no diagnosis' applies to cases WITHOUT those causes. D conflates the two halves into one population — easy to identify + hard to cure — which isn't what the paragraph says. Step 2's split keeps the two cases apart.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-039.technique: Two-half conclusion pattern: when an ELF paragraph lists what IS known and contrasts it with what ISN'T, the right answer uses 'only some' / 'partly' wording. Trigger to memorise: 'But-pivot in a short paragraph → partiality answer'.
var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: 'Easily identified' applied to one subgroup is not the same as 'easy to diagnose' applied to all cases — options D-style traps swap quantifiers between subgroups.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The 'Identifying Authors' passage describes how 'function words' — small, seemingly meaningless items like prepositions, conjunctions and personal pronouns — have long been used to identify anonymous authors, and notes a recent prominent application: unmasking J. K. Rowling as the author of The Cuckoo's Calling. That matches B: superficially trivial linguistic items have traditionally played a key role in them.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is implied concerning authorship identification methods?' — an 'implied' question on the methods themselves (not on any individual case). The right answer should describe what KIND of evidence these methods historically rely on.

steps.title: Locate the method-defining sentences
steps.text: The opening sentence is the methodological anchor: 'Revealing an author by analysing the use of seemingly meaningless bits of language – such as so-called function words, e.g. prepositions, conjunctions and personal pronouns – has a long history.' The Rowling and 'Night Before Christmas' examples are the modern applications of that long-standing method, and the Jackson study used 'high-frequency words like "that" and "the"'.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Authorship-attribution methods look at things you'd never consciously vary as a writer — how often you use 'the', 'that', prepositions, pronouns. These tiny, supposedly meaningless words turn out to fingerprint an author. The technique has been around a long time; recent computer-aided studies on Rowling and 'The Night Before Christmas' are just modern uses of the same idea.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Function words' = grammatical glue words like 'the', 'of', 'and', 'in', 'he', 'she' — they carry little meaning on their own but appear constantly. 'High-frequency words' = words that show up often in a text. 'Authorship attribution' = working out who wrote an anonymous or disputed text. 'Pseudonym' = a fake author name (Rowling used 'Robert Galbraith').

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: B says 'Superficially trivial linguistic items have traditionally played a key role in them' — which translates directly: 'superficially trivial' = 'seemingly meaningless bits'; 'traditionally' = 'has a long history'; 'key role' = the method itself centres on these items. The other options each describe a debate or a development the passage does not endorse.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The insight in one sentence: when an ELF passage opens with a definitional sentence about a method's historical core ('analysing X has a long history'), the implied answer almost always restates that definition in different words.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the Norsworthy quote ('meaningless bits, inconsequential and perhaps random') and infer that critics historically rejected these methods, so today they're LESS controversial than in the 19th century.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage gives a 2016 example of CURRENT controversy (Norsworthy deriding Jackson's analysis) — the controversy is contemporary, not safely retired in the 19th century. Step 2 anchors the controversy in the present. A inverts the temporal direction.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at Norsworthy's criticism and conclude that the method's focus on 'rare words' has been severely criticised.
distractors.why_wrong: The method's focus is on COMMON function words ('that', 'the', prepositions), not RARE words. Step 4's vocabulary note makes this explicit: 'high-frequency words' = frequent ones. C swaps frequent for rare and so misnames the very thing being criticised.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'his computer's use' from the Norsworthy quote and the 2013 / 2016 dates, D — modern computers' precision has revolutionised these methods — sounds like a plausible modern claim.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage uses computers as a CONTEXT for the modern examples but never claims they have 'revolutionised precision' — in fact Norsworthy's mocking quote about the computer points the other way. Step 5 rules D out for promoting a contextual detail to a headline the text does not make.

var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-040.technique: Definitional-opener pattern: when an ELF passage opens with a sentence that DEFINES a method's traditional core ('analysing X has a long history'), the 'implied' answer almost always paraphrases that opener. Trigger to memorise: 'method-defining first sentence → answer that restates the method's traditional ingredients'.
var-2022-1-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: Quote-attractor traps: the most vivid sentence in the paragraph (Norsworthy's mocking quote) pulls attention to a CURRENT debate, but the question asked about the method's traditional character. Always check the opener before the quote.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: The gap connects two clauses with the opposite emotional charge: cats 'flaunt their independence' (they signal they don't need us) but we still bring them home anyway. The connector has to mean 'in spite of'. That's 'Despite' — answer B.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: All four options are PREPOSITIONS or prepositional phrases that introduce a noun: 'Aware of X', 'Despite X', 'Concerning X', 'Owing to X'. So the gap is a sentence-opening connector. You need the one whose LOGICAL RELATION fits the sentence around it.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Full sentence: '___ this hauteur – or perhaps because of it – many of us cannot resist bringing these regal creatures into our homes, litter boxes and all.' The previous sentence said cats 'seem to flaunt their independence, as if to say that they do not really need us.' So the contrast is: cats act haughty, BUT we adopt them anyway.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The aside '– or perhaps because of it –' is the key tell. The writer is offering TWO possible relations to 'hauteur': either we adopt cats in spite of their snootiness, OR because of it. So the main connector must be the 'in spite of' half — only then does 'or perhaps because of it' make sense as an alternative. The required meaning is concessive: although they act standoffish, we still adopt them.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Hauteur' = a haughty, aloof, superior manner (from French 'haut' = high). 'Despite X' = in spite of X; X is true, but something happens anyway — concessive. 'Owing to X' = because of X — causal. 'Concerning X' = on the topic of X — topical. 'Aware of X' = knowing about X — cognitive state; requires a personal subject and doesn't fit a 'many of us cannot resist' construction cleanly.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug B: 'Despite this hauteur – or perhaps because of it – many of us cannot resist…' — reads naturally and the 'or perhaps because of it' aside now makes perfect sense as the opposite causal relation. Plug A: 'Aware of this hauteur' would shift the meaning to 'because we KNOW they're haughty' — and the 'or perhaps because of it' aside doesn't pair with it. Plug C and D similarly fail the aside test.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B — Despite. The insight in one sentence: when an English cloze sentence contains a 'or perhaps because of it' / 'though we might also' aside, the main connector is the OPPOSITE relation that the aside contrasts with — usually a concessive like 'despite'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'this hauteur' is to read it as something we PERCEIVE, so 'Aware of this hauteur' feels like a natural opener — yes, we notice they're haughty.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Aware of' wants a personal subject for which awareness is the verb ('Aware of this hauteur, we still…'), and even then it doesn't pair with the 'or perhaps because of it' aside. Step 5's plug-test shows the aside fails: awareness is a cognitive state, not a relation that can be contrasted with 'because of it'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'this hauteur' as a topic and reach for 'Concerning this hauteur' as a topic-introducer.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Concerning' frames what follows as a discussion ABOUT hauteur ('regarding hauteur, here is what we think…'). The next clause is not a discussion of hauteur — it's a behavioural claim about humans adopting cats. Step 3's relational analysis rules C out: there is no topic-introduction here, there is a concessive contrast.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the aside '– or perhaps because of it –' as a clarification of the MAIN clause, so the main connector should also be causal: 'Owing to this hauteur, we adopt cats'.
distractors.why_wrong: The aside is offered as an ALTERNATIVE to the main connector, not as a clarification: 'or perhaps because of it' announces a competing reading. If the main connector were already causal ('Owing to'), the aside would be redundant. Step 3's tell shows the main connector must be the OPPOSITE — concessive.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-031.technique: Aside-tells-you-the-opposite pattern: when an English cloze has the structure '[connector] X – or perhaps because of X – [main clause]', the connector is the relation that contrasts with the aside. Trigger to memorise: 'or perhaps because of it' aside → main connector is 'despite / in spite of'.
var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: Topical vs concessive prepositions: 'Concerning', 'Regarding' and 'As for' look like generic openers but they introduce a DISCUSSION, not a contrast. Whenever the rest of the sentence makes a contrastive move, those topic-introducers lose.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: The gap names the long process by which prehistoric wildcats turned into modern domestic pets, driven by genes for learning and reduced fearfulness. That's a 'transformation' — answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: All four options are nouns ending in '-tion' describing a CHANGE: reclassification (re-categorising), degeneration (worsening), transformation (turning into something different), procreation (reproducing). So the gap is asking what kind of change a set of genes drove the prehistoric cat through.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Full sentence: 'In 2014, a study identified a set of genes that may have been crucial in the ___ of the prehistoric cat into the cuddly pets we know today.' The preposition 'into' is the key tell: it sets up a 'from X into Y' shape — from prehistoric wildcat, INTO modern house pet.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is 'turning A INTO B', where A is the prehistoric cat and B is today's cuddly pet. The next sentence names the mechanism: 'These genes have been linked to key behavioral traits, such as the ability to learn and reduced fearfulness, which would have helped cats adapt to life in human company.' So the cat actually CHANGED — biologically and behaviourally — over millennia.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Transformation' = a thorough change in form or nature; pairs naturally with 'into' ('the transformation of A into B'). 'Reclassification' = changing the CATEGORY something is filed under, without changing the thing itself; doesn't pair with 'into' a new creature. 'Degeneration' = becoming worse; doesn't fit 'cuddly pets' as the endpoint. 'Procreation' = the production of offspring; doesn't fit 'into the cuddly pets we know'.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug C: 'the transformation of the prehistoric cat into the cuddly pets we know today' — natural, idiomatic, and pairs cleanly with the mechanism (genes for learnability and reduced fear). Plug A: 'reclassification… into cuddly pets' would mean someone changed the LABEL, not the animal. Plug B: 'degeneration into cuddly pets' is contemptuous (degeneration is always pejorative). Plug D: 'procreation… into cuddly pets' doesn't work grammatically — procreation produces, it doesn't turn-into.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C — transformation. The insight in one sentence: when a cloze gap is followed by 'into [endpoint]', look for the noun that names a NATURE-CHANGE process compatible with that endpoint — 'transformation', 'evolution', 'conversion' are the usual winners; categorising verbs lose.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a study about cat 'genes' is taxonomic — scientists re-CATEGORISING the cat from wildcat into a new species or subspecies.
distractors.why_wrong: Reclassification is what HUMAN SCIENTISTS do to LABELS; it doesn't describe the cat actually changing. Step 3's mechanism (behavioural traits, adapting to human company) is about the animal itself shifting, not about taxonomy moving boxes. A picks the human side of the story when the sentence is about the animal's side.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'cuddly pets' and read it as a mild put-down — wildcats were noble hunters, now they're lap-cats — so 'degeneration' might fit a sceptical narrator.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage is unironically affectionate ('the cuddly pets we know today'); there is no put-down to anchor 'degeneration'. Step 4's vocabulary note flags 'degeneration' as always pejorative. B mistakes a neutral tone for a sneering one.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the genetic angle, D — 'procreation' — sounds biologically adjacent (genes, reproduction, offspring).
distractors.why_wrong: Procreation is the ACT of producing offspring, not a process of becoming something different. The sentence frame 'in the ___ of the prehistoric cat INTO the cuddly pets' requires a turning-into noun; 'procreation INTO X' is ungrammatical. Step 5's plug-test fails on the 'into' shape.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-032.technique: Into-shaped gap pattern: when a cloze gap is followed by 'of [start] into [end]', the right answer is a noun for a nature-changing process — transformation, evolution, conversion. Trigger to memorise: 'gap + INTO + endpoint → process noun, not a label noun'.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: The sentence says many mysteries remain about cat behaviour, particularly when set against our 'growing knowledge of dogs'. The gap has to flag a CONTRAST — we know a lot about dogs, but mysteries remain about cats. That's 'in contrast' — answer D.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: The four options are all comparison/contrast connectors: 'parallel' (alike), 'with regard' (topical — 'with regard to X' = on the subject of X), 'similar' (alike), 'in contrast' (the opposite). So the gap is a comparison preposition phrase: 'parallel to', 'with regard to', 'similar to', 'in contrast to' — all end with 'to' before the next noun.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Full sentence: 'Yet many mysteries remain in deciphering feline behavior and intelligence, particularly ___ to our growing knowledge of dogs.' The first clause says we DON'T understand cats well. The second names 'growing knowledge of dogs'. So we have ignorance about cats juxtaposed against ample knowledge about dogs.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is OPPOSITION: little cat-knowledge versus lots of dog-knowledge. The next paragraph confirms this — it explains WHY we know more about dogs (urgent social problems, pack behaviour studied) and less about cats (solitary ancestors, lab-unfriendly). So the connector has to mark a comparison where the two sides DIFFER.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'In contrast to X' = differing from X; sets up an opposition. 'Parallel to X' / 'similar to X' = like X, alongside X; same shape. 'With regard to X' = on the subject of X; topical, not contrastive. The bracket of options is built to test whether you can tell a contrast from a parallel — choose only after you've named the relationship explicitly.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug D: 'mysteries remain in deciphering feline behavior, particularly in contrast to our growing knowledge of dogs' — natural, and the contrast is exactly the writer's point. Plug A or C: 'parallel to' / 'similar to our growing knowledge of dogs' would claim cat and dog knowledge are SIMILAR — the opposite of the writer's claim. Plug B: 'with regard to our growing knowledge of dogs' is grammatically fine but says the cat mysteries are ABOUT dog-knowledge, which is incoherent.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D — in contrast. The insight in one sentence: when an English cloze sets up 'we don't know much about X… ___ to our growing knowledge of Y', the only relation that fits is contrast — 'in contrast to', 'unlike', 'as opposed to'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'cats and dogs' is to compare them as pet companions — 'parallel to our growing knowledge of dogs' sounds like it's just placing the two fields side by side.
distractors.why_wrong: Parallel = ALIKE in the relevant respect; but the writer's claim is precisely that cat knowledge is BEHIND dog knowledge. Step 3's identified relationship is opposition, not equivalence. A reads the sentence as a neutral pairing when it is a deliberate contrast.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'particularly' and reach for the topic-introducer 'with regard to' — it's the most generic comparison phrase, so it feels safe.
distractors.why_wrong: 'With regard to our growing knowledge of dogs' makes the dog-knowledge the TOPIC the cat mysteries pertain to — which is logically backwards. Step 5's plug test produces an incoherent reading. B is a generic-phrase trap.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'similar to our growing knowledge of dogs' as 'similarly, there is mystery about both' — making the connector 'similar to' a way of LINKING two fields.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Similar to X' compares the FOLLOWING NOUN to X. Here the comparison would be 'mysteries' similar to 'growing knowledge' — which clashes (mysteries ≠ knowledge). The sentence wants a relationship between the cat side and the dog side, with opposite directions. Step 4's vocabulary note pins down the difference.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-033.technique: Contrast-of-knowledge pattern: when a cloze says 'mysteries remain about X… ___ to our growing/abundant knowledge of Y', the answer is a contrast preposition ('in contrast to'). Trigger to memorise: 'don't know about X + know about Y → in contrast to'.
var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: Generic-phrase trap: 'with regard to' is so common it slips into any gap that looks like a connector. It loses whenever the sentence demands a CONTRAST rather than a topic-introduction.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: The gap describes the wildcat ancestors of the house cat, contrasted with dogs' ancestors who 'roamed in packs and learned sophisticated strategies for interacting socially'. The wildcat opposite of pack-living and sociable is 'solitary' — answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: The four options are adjectives describing the wildcat's social or behavioural type: 'domestic' (kept by humans), 'original' (first / primary), 'solitary' (alone), 'extrovert' (outgoing). So the gap is asking what KIND of animal the wildcat progenitor was, compared to the dog's pack-living ancestor.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Full sentence: 'Whereas the ancestors of dogs roamed in packs and learned sophisticated strategies for interacting socially with other animals, the wildcat progenitors of today's house cat were more ___ animals.' 'Whereas' is the explicit contrast marker — the wildcat side must be the OPPOSITE of pack-living + socially sophisticated.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Whereas-clauses set up a one-to-one contrast: dog ancestors lived in PACKS and interacted SOCIALLY; wildcat ancestors must live ALONE and interact LESS. So the gap's adjective has to mean 'living alone, not in groups' — that's textbook 'solitary' in animal-behaviour writing.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Solitary' (of an animal) = living alone, not in a group — the standard ecological term that opposes 'pack' or 'social'. 'Domestic' = kept by or living with humans. 'Original' = first / earliest. 'Extrovert' = outgoing, sociable — the OPPOSITE of what the contrast requires. The wildcat field also strongly cues 'solitary' as the textbook descriptor (think of how cats are described in nature documentaries).

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug C: '…the wildcat progenitors of today's house cat were more solitary animals.' Clean opposition to 'dog ancestors roamed in packs and learned… social strategies'. The next sentence confirms it: 'The patterns that govern their exchanges with one another and humans are therefore harder to parse' — exactly the consequence of having a solitary ancestor. Plug A: 'more domestic' contradicts 'wildcat progenitors' (wild ≠ domestic). Plug B: 'more original' makes no sense in a behavioural contrast. Plug D: 'more extrovert' is the opposite of what 'whereas… packs and social strategies' demands.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C — solitary. The insight in one sentence: 'whereas' clauses make the gap's job mechanical — find the adjective that is the direct opposite of the contrasting clause's key claim; here pack-living + social → solitary.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'today's house cat' is the word 'domestic' — house cats are the domestic species, so 'domestic animals' sounds adjacent.
distractors.why_wrong: The gap describes WILDCAT progenitors, not today's house cat. Wild ≠ domestic by definition. Step 5's plug-test fails directly: 'wildcat progenitors… were more domestic animals' is self-contradictory.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'progenitors of today's house cat' and reach for 'original' — 'original animals' as in 'the original ancestors'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Original' adds no behavioural information, and the sentence is set up by 'whereas' to provide a behavioural contrast. Step 3's relational analysis rules out any adjective that doesn't oppose 'pack-living + social'. B is a redundant adjective rather than a contrastive one.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read 'more sophisticated strategies for interacting socially' as the contrast target, you might pick the closest synonym of 'social' — 'extrovert'.
distractors.why_wrong: But 'whereas' DEMANDS the opposite, not a synonym. Step 4's note locks in the rule: in 'whereas A… were P, B was more Q', P and Q oppose each other. 'Extrovert' is the same-side word; 'solitary' is the opposite-side word.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-034.technique: Whereas-opposition pattern: 'whereas' clauses give you a one-to-one contrast — the gap's adjective is the direct opposite of the prior clause's key descriptor. Trigger to memorise: 'whereas A did P, B was more ___' → ___ is the opposite of P.
var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: Same-side traps: when the contrasting clause names a TRAIT ('social', 'pack-living'), distractors will offer either a synonym (extrovert) or an adjacent property (domestic). The gap always wants the OPPOSITE — choose only after explicitly naming what the opposite has to be.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: The sentence acknowledges that cat-research is hampered by lab-difficulties and a reliance on home observation, yet still has more to learn. The gap should mean 'taking these complications into account' or 'accepting that these complications exist'. That's 'given' — answer A.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: All four options are participle / preposition-style openers that introduce a noun phrase: 'given X', 'compared to X', 'ignoring X', 'according to X'. The gap is asking how the writer wants you to relate the named 'complications' to the conclusion.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Full sentence: 'Thus, ___ these complications, we still have much to learn about cats.' The word 'still' is the tell. 'Still' means 'in spite of what just came'. So the gap has to MENTION the complications (not erase them), in a way compatible with 'still we have much to learn'.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The relationship is 'acknowledging the difficulties AND adding that there's a lot left to learn'. 'Given X' = taking X into account, accepting X as fact, and then drawing a conclusion. So 'Given these complications, we still have much to learn' = 'Accepting these complications exist, there is nonetheless much to learn' — exactly the writer's move.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Given X' = taking X as given / since X is the case; sets X up as accepted background for a following claim. 'Ignoring X' = setting X aside, NOT taking it into account — the opposite of what we want. 'Compared to X' = drawing a comparison between something and X. 'According to X' = on the authority of X — wants X to be a source or claim, not a list of difficulties.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug A: 'Thus, given these complications, we still have much to learn about cats' — reads naturally; 'still' makes sense because we've already accepted that complications make the research hard. Plug B: 'compared to these complications, we still have much to learn' — incoherent (comparison to what?). Plug C: 'ignoring these complications, we still have much to learn' — contradicts 'still'; if we ignore the difficulties, we don't need 'still'. Plug D: 'according to these complications' — treats difficulties as a SOURCE of information, which they aren't.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A — given. The insight in one sentence: closing-summary cloze gaps usually want a 'taking-into-account' preposition ('given', 'in light of', 'despite') — pick the one whose tone matches the rest of the sentence's hedged 'still'.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a closing sentence following a list of obstacles is to compare the obstacles to something — 'compared to these complications, our progress is modest'.
distractors.why_wrong: The sentence does not name a SECOND term to compare against. 'Compared to X' needs both terms; here only X (the complications) is present. Step 5's plug test fails — the comparison has nothing to anchor to.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'still have much to learn' as 'we're pressing on anyway' and reach for 'ignoring these complications'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Ignoring' would mean we are SETTING THE COMPLICATIONS ASIDE — but the whole previous paragraph has been DESCRIBING the complications. Step 3's relation is acknowledgment-then-conclusion, not dismissal. Worse: with 'ignoring', 'still' becomes redundant — if we ignore the obstacles, 'still' has nothing to push against.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the structural 'Thus, ___' opener and reach for 'according to' as a generic source-introducer.
distractors.why_wrong: 'According to' requires its object to be a SOURCE of a claim — a person, a study, a theory — not a list of difficulties. Step 4's vocabulary note locks in the rule. D is a connector-shape mismatch.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-035.technique: Hedge-keeping pattern: when a closing sentence opens with 'Thus, ___ these [difficulties / complications / obstacles], we STILL …', the connector is 'given' or 'in light of' — preserve the difficulties as part of the conclusion's background. Trigger to memorise: 'Thus + still in the main clause → acknowledging-preposition in the gap'.
var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: The 'still' check: any candidate that makes 'still' redundant ('ignoring these complications, we still have much to learn') is automatically wrong — 'still' only earns its place when the gap preserves the obstacle.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The passage says the 1878 Paris exhibition's prehistoric artifacts 'dominated his thoughts upon his return', and reminded Sautuola of bone fragments he'd seen in a local cave — which is what drove him to revisit Altamira. The exhibition opened his eyes to what his own finds might be. That matches B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is implied in connection with the 1878 exhibition in Paris?' — an 'implied' question. You're not looking for a direct quote about the exhibition's importance, but for the option the passage's logic supports. Watch for distractors that promote the exhibition to roles the text doesn't give it.

steps.title: Locate the key sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the exhibition's role. First: 'The prehistoric artifacts on display dominated his thoughts upon his return to his country estate near the medieval city of Santillana del Mar near Spain's northern coast.' Then: 'Sautuola started to think more and more about some bone fragments he had seen in a local cave a few years before.' And: 'Sautuola and his young daughter, María, visited the cave of Altamira the summer after his Paris visit.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Sautuola goes to Paris in 1878. He sees prehistoric artifacts there. They stay on his mind. They remind him of bone fragments he'd seen years earlier in a cave near his home. The very next summer he goes back to that cave with his daughter — and they find the paintings. So the exhibition is what made him SEE his old finds as potentially prehistoric.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Universal Exhibition' = a 19th-century international fair where countries showed off industry, art and science (the 1878 Paris one was huge). 'Prehistoric artifacts' = objects made before written records. 'Amateur archaeologist' = someone who pursues archaeology without being a paid professional. The point of the exhibition for Sautuola is that it gave him a CATEGORY for what he had already seen.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: B says 'It opened de Sautuola's eyes to the possible nature of some of his own finds.' That maps onto Step 3's chain: artifacts on display → preoccupation → revisiting old cave fragments in a new light → cave trip the following summer. The exhibition is the trigger that recategorised his old bone fragments as potentially prehistoric.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The insight in one sentence: 'implied' questions on a biographical-cause sentence pair reward the option that names the EFFECT-OF-CAUSE (the exhibition's role in changing how he saw his own past finds), not the most literal restatement of any single sentence.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'fascinated by what he saw at the Universal Exhibition' and assume Sautuola was a SHOWER at the exhibition, displaying his own cave finds to a research community.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says Sautuola was a viewer, not an exhibitor: 'fascinated by what he SAW'. The Altamira finds in fact don't come until AFTER Paris. Step 3's timeline makes this concrete — Paris in 1878, cave revisit in summer 1879. A inverts who is showing whom.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the strong wording 'dominated his thoughts upon his return' and conclude that Paris started his interest in archaeology and human prehistory from scratch.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says he was already an 'amateur archaeologist' BEFORE Paris and had already seen bone fragments in a local cave 'a few years before'. Step 4's vocabulary note pins this down: 'amateur archaeologist' is his profile when he arrives in Paris, not a result of the visit. C overstates the exhibition's role as the starting point.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the chronology — old bone fragments + Paris artifacts + later cave paintings — D ('it gave him a chance to compare paintings he had seen with similar discoveries') sounds plausible: a comparison.
distractors.why_wrong: He had not yet seen the cave PAINTINGS at the time of the Paris visit — only bone fragments. The paintings come in summer 1879, after Paris. Step 5's chain order rules D out: comparing paintings to similar discoveries would require having seen the paintings first.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-036.technique: Trigger-event pattern: when an ELF biographical passage shows X visiting Y and then revisiting earlier work in a new light, an 'implied' question about Y's role rewards the option that names Y as the RE-INTERPRETATION TRIGGER, not the starting point. Trigger to memorise: 'visit → return → reinterpret old finds → trigger-event answer'.
var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: Chronological discipline: the cave PAINTINGS are discovered AFTER the Paris visit. Any option that has Sautuola comparing paintings at Paris (D) or showing his own paintings there (A) is breaking the timeline.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The passage describes Sautuola as 'bitterly disappointed by the skepticism' and enduring 'more humiliation' when accused of hiring a hoaxer. He never doubts the paintings himself — he simply suffers from being dismissed. That matches C: he was hurt by the fact that they did not support his conclusions.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks about de Sautuola's reaction to the verdict (parsed text says 'svaret' — that's a Swedish word that slipped into the OCR; in context it means 'verdict / answer / ruling') of Cartailhac and the delegates. You need the option that captures HIS EMOTIONAL AND PROFESSIONAL response, not the verdict itself.

steps.title: Locate the reaction sentences
steps.text: Three sentences carry his reaction. 'From the moment of his discovery, Sautuola was convinced the artwork was ancient, but his joy was short-lived.' Then: 'Bitterly disappointed by the skepticism of the archaeologists, Sautuola endured more humiliation when it was suggested that he had hired a local artist to create an elaborate hoax.' Note: 'convinced the artwork was ancient' is steady throughout — his belief doesn't waver.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Sautuola believed the paintings were genuine prehistoric art from the moment he saw them. The 1880 Lisbon congress, led by Cartailhac, dismissed them. Then critics went further and accused him of hiring an artist to fake them. He was 'bitterly disappointed' and 'humiliated' — but he never changed his mind about the paintings. He was hurt that the experts rejected his conclusion, not that the conclusion itself was wrong.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Bitterly disappointed' = deeply hurt, with lasting resentment. 'Humiliation' = public shame or loss of dignity. 'Hoax' = an elaborate deception staged to fool people. 'Hire a local artist to create an elaborate hoax' = the accusation that Sautuola PAID someone to paint the cave walls fraudulently. The emotional register is hurt-but-resolute, not capitulating.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: C says 'He was hurt by the fact that they did not support his conclusions.' That captures both halves: HURT ('bitterly disappointed', 'humiliation') and HE STILL HOLDS HIS CONCLUSIONS (the passage shows him steady from 'the moment of his discovery'). The other options each break one of those halves.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The insight in one sentence: when an ELF passage stresses both lasting pain AND lasting conviction, the right answer must include BOTH — hurt without surrender, not hurt-and-withdrawal or hurt-and-doubt.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'bitterly disappointed' + 'humiliation' as career-ending and conclude he 'withdrew from archaeology for fear of making a fool of himself'.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage gives no withdrawal — and the closing of the article shows his daughter still meeting Cartailhac in 1902, who issues a 'Mea Culpa' acknowledging the error. Step 3's paraphrase preserves Sautuola's steady belief. A invents a retreat the text doesn't record.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the strong negative emotional language and conclude that the social shame must have made him doubt the paintings' authenticity.
distractors.why_wrong: The very next clause after introducing his belief — 'Sautuola was convinced the artwork was ancient' — anchors the whole arc. The hurt and the humiliation never touch the conviction. Step 2's quote is the anchor. B confuses emotional disappointment with intellectual doubt.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'convinced the artwork was ancient', D — 'so confident that he cared little about their views' — sounds consistent with his unwavering conviction.
distractors.why_wrong: Confidence and indifference are not the same thing. The passage uses 'bitterly disappointed' and 'humiliation' — these are exactly opposite to 'cared little'. Step 5's two-halves rule: conviction yes, indifference no. D drops the hurt half.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-037.technique: Steady-belief-but-hurt pattern: when an ELF passage records lasting emotional damage from rejection WHILE preserving the subject's intellectual conviction, the right answer must hold BOTH (hurt + still believes). Trigger to memorise: 'bitter disappointment + unchanged conviction → hurt-without-surrender answer'.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: The passage says that between 1890 and 1901 'more discoveries of cave art in Europe began casting doubt on the scholars' attitudes', the new finds 'confirmed' Paleolithic-era artistry, and 'attitudes began to change'. That matches A: the finds led to increasing acceptance of de Sautuola's discoveries.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What are we told about the discoveries of additional cave paintings in the late 19th century?' This is a content-summary question about a SPECIFIC PERIOD (late 19th century, after the Lisbon dismissal). Focus on the passage's chronology block from the 1880 congress to 1901.

steps.title: Locate the late-19th-century block
steps.text: The relevant sentences: 'However, between 1890 and 1901, more discoveries of cave art in Europe began casting doubt on the scholars' attitudes. The spate of other finds confirmed that there were people living well back into the last glacial period, like those at Altamira, who were capable of creating a vibrant body of artwork. New questions were raised about the cave at Altamira, and attitudes began to change.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Between 1890 and 1901, more cave-painting finds turned up across Europe. These confirmed that prehistoric people really could make sophisticated art. That accumulation undermined the dismissive Lisbon-era position. Scholars started reopening the Altamira case and changing their minds. So the late-19th-century finds steadily moved the field toward accepting Sautuola.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Spate of other finds' = a flood of additional discoveries in quick succession. 'Casting doubt on the scholars' attitudes' = making the experts' position look wrong. 'Last glacial period' = the ice age that ended about 10,000 years ago — a Paleolithic-era timeframe. 'Attitudes began to change' = the dismissal of Altamira began to soften.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: A says 'The finds led to increasing acceptance of the authenticity of de Sautuola's discoveries.' The passage's chain of 'casting doubt on scholars' attitudes' + 'attitudes began to change' is exactly this gradual reversal. 'Increasing acceptance' captures the gradual nature ('began to'). The other options each either invent a fact or reverse the direction.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. The insight in one sentence: when an ELF passage uses 'began to change' / 'casting doubt' language about a period of new evidence, the right answer must capture GRADUAL movement TOWARD the previously rejected view — not a sudden break or continued rejection.

distractors.why_tempting: Earlier in the article, scholars accused Sautuola of staging an 'elaborate hoax' with a hired local artist. It's easy to think that pattern continued and that the late-19th-century finds were ALSO suspected pranks.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'hoax' accusation is specifically about ALTAMIRA in the 1880s. The 1890-1901 finds are described as legitimate 'spate of other finds' that CONFIRMED Paleolithic artistry. Step 2's quote is unambiguous. B carries a 1880s suspicion forward to a period where the passage explicitly drops it.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the line about scholars being skeptical of Altamira and assume the academic community was equally skeptical of the new finds.
distractors.why_wrong: The 1890-1901 finds did the OPPOSITE: they 'cast doubt on the scholars' attitudes', meaning they undermined the dismissal. Step 3's paraphrase makes the direction clear. C reverses the direction of the doubt — it was the SCHOLARS who started doubting, not the finds being doubted.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'use of perspective, creation of pigments using water or fat mixtures, and use of a paintbrush-like tool' from the earlier paragraph, D — that archaeologists were 'confused by the lack of tools and other objects in these caves' — sounds vaguely on-topic.
distractors.why_wrong: The earlier passage actually names the TOOLS involved (water/fat mixtures, brush-like implement); it never says tools were missing. And the 1890-1901 paragraph never raises the question of tools at all — it talks about ATTITUDES changing. Step 5 rules D out for inventing a complaint the period-specific text doesn't make.
