var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-038.technique: Gradual-acceptance pattern: when an ELF passage describes accumulating evidence over a date range ('between X and Y… spate of finds… attitudes began to change'), the right answer names the gradual REVERSAL of the prior consensus. Trigger to memorise: 'began to change + spate of finds → increasing acceptance'.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-039.solution_path: Today archaeologists date the bulk of Altamira's paintings to 14,000–17,000 years old, while ALSO noting that ocher curves on the main ceiling were made over 35,000 years ago, with related cave art elsewhere possibly older than 40,000 years. That's a very long span of activity, matching D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is the modern view among archaeologists with regard to the Altamira cave paintings?' The 'modern view' part directs you to the present-tense block in the passage (the 'Today, archaeologists have established…' paragraph). Focus your search there.

steps.title: Locate the modern-view block
steps.text: Key sentences from that paragraph: 'Today, archaeologists have established that the bulk of the artwork in the cave is between 14,000 and 17,000 years old. Designated a UNESCO World Heritage site since 1985, Altamira also contains some of the earliest Stone Age painting ever found. Over 35,000 years ago, someone entered the Altamira cave with a natural yellow-orange pigment (ocher) and water, and, using their fingers, traced various parallel curves on the main ceiling near the cave mouth.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Most of the cave's paintings are 14,000–17,000 years old. But there is also much older work — parallel ocher curves traced over 35,000 years ago. So Altamira wasn't decorated in one short burst; people came back at very different times across a span of more than 20,000 years. That long timespan is the modern view.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Ocher' = a natural yellow-to-orange-red pigment made from iron-oxide-rich earth — the oldest pigment used by humans. 'Bulk of the artwork' = MOST of the paintings, not all. 'Stone Age' here = Paleolithic (the older Stone Age). Two distinct dates matter: ~14,000-17,000 ya for most paintings; ~35,000+ ya for some early ocher tracings.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: D says 'They seem to have been created during a very long time span.' That maps exactly onto Step 3's two-date paraphrase: most work ~14,000-17,000 years ago, earliest tracings 35,000+ years ago — at least 20,000 years between earliest and latest. The 'long time span' wording captures the multi-millennium spread.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The insight in one sentence: when an ELF 'modern view' question follows a passage giving TWO distinct ages for material in the same site, the right answer is the 'long time span' option — not the option that picks one age over the other.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on '14,000–17,000 years old' and '35,000 years ago' is that there is an academic dispute between two age camps: 15,000 vs 35,000.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't present these as competing claims — it presents them as DIFFERENT layers of work at the same site, both established as fact. Step 3's two-date paraphrase keeps both dates true. A treats agreement-over-a-span as if it were disagreement-over-age.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Altamira also contains some of the earliest Stone Age painting ever found' and conclude Altamira holds THE oldest paintings in Europe.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Some of the earliest' ≠ 'the oldest yet to be found'. The very next sentence says 'art found in other caves in the same region of Spain could date to more than 40,000 years ago' — older than Altamira's 35,000. Step 4's vocabulary discipline rules out the superlative. B promotes 'some of the earliest' to 'the oldest'.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read the line about 'The earliest paintings found in the Chauvet Cave, discovered in France's Ardeche gorges in 1994', C — fundamental differences between Spanish and French cave art — sounds like a comparison the passage is making.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't draw fundamental differences. It actually emphasises CONTINUITY: the Chauvet finds are 'a little later – around 33,000 to 32,000 years ago', placing them in the same broad timeframe as Altamira's earliest tracings. Step 5's match remains the long-time-span reading. C invents a Spain-vs-France contrast the passage doesn't make.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-039.technique: Span-not-spot pattern: when an ELF passage gives two distinct dates for material in the same archaeological site, the 'modern view' answer is the one that names the SPAN of activity ('a very long time span', 'over many millennia'), not the one that picks one date or sets up a debate. Trigger to memorise: 'two distinct dates at one site → long-time-span answer'.
var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-039.pitfall: 'Some of the earliest' vs 'the oldest': adjectives of partiality ('some', 'among the') in the passage block superlative answers ('the oldest', 'the first') in the options.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-040.solution_path: The passage names 'one clue of extraordinary importance: the use of the rock itself, the way the little imperfections of the walls and ceilings become part of the complete work'. The painters were INCORPORATING the stone's natural shape into the painting. That matches C: the creation of the paintings involved using the shape of the stone in the cave.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is implied about the process of making the cave paintings?' This zooms in on the artistic METHOD, not the dating, the discovery, or the painters' identity. Focus on the paragraph that asks 'Why, or for what purpose, were these cave paintings made?' — that's where the process clue lives.

steps.title: Locate the process-clue sentence
steps.text: The decisive sentence: 'There is one clue of extraordinary importance: the use of the rock itself, the way the little imperfections of the walls and ceilings become part of the complete work.' Followed by: 'Does the art represent a union between life and the inert rock, a connection between the figures created and their natural context…'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: When the prehistoric artists painted on the cave walls, they didn't treat the rock as a flat blank surface. They used the natural bumps, ridges and curves of the wall — its 'imperfections' — and incorporated them into the painted figures. So a bulge in the rock might become the curve of a bison's flank. The stone's shape became part of the artwork.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Imperfections of the walls and ceilings' = the natural irregularities of the rock surface — its bumps, hollows, ridges, curves. 'Become part of the complete work' = the painters didn't paint over those irregularities; they USED them. The earlier paragraph noted the painters' kit: water-or-fat-based pigments, perspective, a brush-like tool. None of that conflicts with the use-the-rock observation — they were sophisticated painters who responded to the surface they had.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.text: C says 'The creation of the paintings involved using the shape of the stone in the cave.' That maps directly onto 'the use of the rock itself, the way the little imperfections of the walls and ceilings become part of the complete work'. Both halves of C are anchored: 'using the shape' = 'use of the rock itself'; 'in the cave' = the wall-and-ceiling locus.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The insight in one sentence: when an ELF passage flags 'one clue of extraordinary importance' and then names a SURFACE-or-TECHNIQUE detail, the implied process answer is the one that paraphrases that surface detail.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'use of the rock itself' as 'cutting into the rock' or 'carving the rock' — a stone-on-stone technique.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage describes PAINTING with pigment (ocher, water, fat-mixtures, brush-like tool, fingers). Step 4's vocabulary note lists the painting kit. Nothing in the passage says they carved. A misreads 'use of the rock' as a carving claim when it actually means 'painting THAT INCORPORATES the rock's shape'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'imperfections of the walls' and reach for 'the hardness of the rock was particularly suitable for art creation' — sounds like a fitting compliment to the cave.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage praises the IRREGULARITY (bumps, hollows), not the HARDNESS. Step 3's paraphrase pins the praise to surface SHAPE, not mineral properties. B promotes a different property of stone (its hardness) into the headline.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'creation of pigments using water or fat mixtures', D — pigment partly made from particles of the cave's own rock — sounds chemically adjacent.
distractors.why_wrong: The pigment the passage names is 'a natural yellow-orange pigment (ocher)' — sourced from iron-oxide earth, not from rock chipped off the cave walls. The passage never says the cave's rock contributed to the pigment chemistry. Step 5's anchor is geometric ('use of the SHAPE'), not chemical. D conflates pigment material with surface shape.

var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-040.technique: Flagged-clue pattern: when an ELF passage announces 'one clue of extraordinary importance' followed by a specific physical detail, the implied-process answer paraphrases THAT FLAGGED DETAIL. Trigger to memorise: 'clue of extraordinary importance + physical detail → process answer = paraphrase of detail'.
var-2022-1-verb2-ELF-040.pitfall: Synonym-trap on 'rock': 'use of the rock' invites confusion between (a) the rock's SHAPE being incorporated into a painted image and (b) the rock being CARVED, or (c) the rock supplying the pigment. The passage is unambiguously (a).
